turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've had a persistent cough for about 5 days now, and it just doesn't seem to be getting better. It started out dry but then became productive with yellowish-green sputum. I've been taking OTC dextromethorphan to try and relieve it, but that hasn't helped much either.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's not just the cough that's bothering me – I'm also experiencing difficulty breathing and wheezing when I try to do even simple tasks, like carrying groceries up the stairs. And on top of that, I've felt really fatigued and run down, which has caused me to miss three days of work already.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: When I'm wheezing and feeling short of breath, my chest feels tight and it's hard for me to catch my breath, even after just a few minutes of activity. And sometimes, in the evenings when I'm lying down, I get this low-grade fever that lasts for a few hours – it's around 38.2 degrees Celsius, not super high but it's definitely there.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: Yes, actually, another thing I've been experiencing is night sweats - I've soaked through my pajamas every single night for the past four nights. It's really frustrating because I'm trying to get some rest and take care of myself, but this sweating is making it hard to sleep comfortably.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, actually, there was one specific instance where I did experience some profuse sweating, which was during those night sweats. But aside from that, I haven't noticed any other sweating or clamminess when my symptoms are active, such as during wheezing or shortness of breath.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: To be honest, the tightness and wheezing seem to be mostly centered in my chest area. Sometimes it radiates up towards my throat when I'm coughing, but generally speaking, it feels like it's localized to my chest when I'm experiencing those symptoms.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: It mostly stays in my chest area, but sometimes when I cough really hard, it can feel a bit in my upper back. But it doesn't usually go down into my arms or anything, just kind of localized to my torso.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I don't experience any tingling or numbness in my arms. It mostly stays confined to my chest area and upper back when I'm feeling those symptoms.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, I don't experience any tingling or numbness in my arms. My hands and fingers feel completely normal, and I haven't noticed any unusual sensations like burning or aching in them either.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: To be honest, I don't think so. I mean, my feet and ankles have been feeling pretty normal to me. I haven't really paid much attention to them since this whole thing started, but if anything's going on, it doesn't seem like swelling or anything like that.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: I don't think so. I mean, my feet and ankles have been feeling pretty normal to me. I haven't really paid much attention to them since this whole thing started, but if anything's going on, it doesn't seem like swelling or anything like that.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really experienced any significant stomach issues since this started. However, I have noticed that taking the OTC dextromethorphan has given me a bit of an upset stomach when I take it on an empty stomach, but that's about it. Nothing too severe, just a mild queasiness for a short while after taking the medication.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Honestly, no, I don't usually feel sick to my stomach during the episodes. Like I said, the main issues have been with my cough, breathing, and fatigue. Occasionally, taking the dextromethorphan can give me a bit of an upset stomach when I haven't eaten beforehand, but that's not related to when my symptoms are active.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: Yes, I've been experiencing low-grade fever, around 38.2 degrees Celsius, and I've also had some night sweats - I've soaked through my pajamas every single night for the past four nights. It's really frustrating because it's making it hard to sleep comfortably.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: I have been experiencing a decrease in appetite over the past 5 days, which is making it even harder for me to cope with all of these symptoms. I'm not really hungry at all, and when I do try to eat, nothing seems to taste good or feel satisfying.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: To be honest, my lifestyle hasn't been great lately. As an elementary school teacher, I'm on my feet all day and deal with young children every day, which can be exhausting. When I'm not working, I've been trying to take care of myself by getting enough rest, but it's hard when I'm having trouble sleeping due to the night sweats and discomfort from my symptoms. As for diet, I try to eat healthy most of the time, but with a decreased appetite lately, I haven't been eating as much as usual. I do enjoy drinking coffee and tea, and I've probably had more of those than usual in an attempt to stay awake during the day. Exercise-wise, I usually try to get some physical activity by walking my dog or doing light yoga at home, but with how I've been feeling lately, it's been really hard to motivate myself to do that.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: I'm actually quite active, both physically and mentally. As an elementary school teacher, I'm on my feet all day teaching and managing a classroom full of energetic young children. It can be exhausting, but it's also rewarding. In my free time, I usually try to get some physical activity by walking my dog or doing light yoga at home. However, with how I've been feeling lately, it's been really hard to motivate myself to do that. I feel like even simple tasks leave me breathless and fatigued, so exercise has become a real challenge for me right now.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my appetite has been really off lately. I'd say it's decreased significantly over the past 5 days. Normally, I'm a big breakfast person and enjoy starting my day with something hearty, but even that hasn't sounded appetizing to me lately. When I do try to eat, it feels like nothing tastes good or feels satisfying. If anything, I've been sticking to light snacks and liquids just to keep my energy up throughout the day.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: To be honest, my mom had some lung issues when she was younger, but nothing too serious. She had some chronic bronchitis and asthma-like symptoms, but she managed it with medication and lifestyle changes. I'm not really aware of any other major medical problems in my family that might be relevant to what's going on with me right now.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My mom actually has asthma, and she's been managing it for a while now. She's always been pretty open about her health history, so I've learned a bit from her experiences with lung issues.
